stem: Let \( u = \begin{bmatrix} [[a]] \\ [[b]] \end{bmatrix} \) and \( v = \begin{bmatrix} [[c]] \\ [[d]] \end{bmatrix} \), and form the rank-one matrix \( M = u\,v^{\mathsf{T}} = \begin{bmatrix} [[e11]] & [[e12]] \\ [[e21]] & [[e22]] \end{bmatrix} \). What is the largest eigenvalue of \( M \)?
solution: A rank-one matrix \( M = u\,v^{\mathsf{T}} \) has eigenvalues \( 0 \) and \( v^{\mathsf{T}}u = [[a]]\cdot[[c]] + [[b]]\cdot[[d]] \) (its trace). Since that value is positive here, the largest eigenvalue is \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the other eigenvalue of a rank-one matrix, not the largest one.
This is only the first product \( ac \); the nonzero eigenvalue is \( ac + bd \).
This is only the second product \( bd \); the nonzero eigenvalue is \( ac + bd \).
This sums the off-diagonal products \( ad + bc \) instead of the trace \( ac + bd \).
This subtracts the two products instead of adding them.
This is one more than the correct largest eigenvalue.
This is one less than the correct largest eigenvalue.

stem: Let \( u = \begin{bmatrix} [[a]] \\ [[b]] \end{bmatrix} \) and \( v = \begin{bmatrix} [[c]] \\ [[d]] \end{bmatrix} \), and let \( M = u\,v^{\mathsf{T}} \). What is \( \operatorname{tr}(M) \)?
solution: For a rank-one matrix \( M = u\,v^{\mathsf{T}} \), the trace equals the dot product \( v^{\mathsf{T}}u = [[a]]\cdot[[c]] + [[b]]\cdot[[d]] = [[answer]] \); this is also its only nonzero eigenvalue.
distractor_rationales: The trace of a rank-one matrix is not automatically zero; it equals \( u\cdot v \), which need not vanish.
This sums the off-diagonal products \( ad+bc \) instead of the diagonal products \( ac+bd \).
This subtracts the two diagonal products instead of adding them.
This multiplies all four entries together instead of summing the diagonal ones.
This adds the raw vector entries instead of the pairwise products that form the diagonal.
This is one more than the correct trace.
This is two less than the correct trace.

stem: Let \( I \) be the \( 2\times 2 \) identity matrix, \( u = \begin{bmatrix} [[a]] \\ [[b]] \end{bmatrix} \), \( v = \begin{bmatrix} [[p]] \\ [[q]] \end{bmatrix} \), and \( A = [[k]] I + u\,v^{\mathsf{T}} = \begin{bmatrix} [[e11]] & [[e12]] \\ [[e21]] & [[e22]] \end{bmatrix} \). What is the largest eigenvalue of \( A \)?
solution: A rank-one update of a scalar matrix, \( A = kI + u\,v^{\mathsf{T}} \), has eigenvalues \( k \) (from any vector orthogonal to \( v \)) and \( k + v^{\mathsf{T}}u = [[k]] + [[a]]\cdot[[p]] + [[b]]\cdot[[q]] \) (from the eigenvector \( u \)). The larger of these two is \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is only the base scalar \( k \), which is not always the larger eigenvalue.
This is only the update term \( v^{\mathsf{T}}u \), omitting the base scalar \( k \).
This subtracts the update term from \( k \) instead of adding it.
This uses only one entry of the outer product instead of the full dot product \( u\cdot v \).
This double-counts the scalar shift \( k \).
This is one more than the correct largest eigenvalue.
This is one less than the correct largest eigenvalue.

stem: Let \( u = \begin{bmatrix} [[a]] \\ [[b]] \end{bmatrix} \) and \( v = \begin{bmatrix} [[c]] \\ [[d]] \end{bmatrix} \), and let \( M = u\,v^{\mathsf{T}} = \begin{bmatrix} [[e11]] & [[e12]] \\ [[e21]] & [[e22]] \end{bmatrix} \). What is \( \det(M) \)?
solution: Every rank-one matrix \( M = u\,v^{\mathsf{T}} \) is singular (its columns are all multiples of \( u \)), so \( \det(M) = [[e11]]\cdot[[e22]] - [[e12]]\cdot[[e21]] = [[answer]] \) regardless of the choice of \( u, v \).
distractor_rationales: This multiplies all four matrix entries together instead of computing \( ad-bc \) on \( M \)'s entries.
This treats \( a,b,c,d \) themselves as the matrix entries and computes their determinant, not \( \det(M) \).
This adds the diagonal products of \( M \) instead of subtracting them, which does not simplify to \( 0 \).
This is one less than a nonzero product, not the determinant of a rank-one matrix.
A rank-one matrix cannot have determinant \( 1 \); it is always singular.
A rank-one matrix cannot have determinant \( -1 \); it is always singular.
